serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately 350 g (one bowl), as commonly served at Singaporean hawker centres and food courts.

nutrient_source.title: Fried fish soup (with milk) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Fried fish soup (with milk)', cr_id F03020015. All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried fish soup with evaporated milk. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the analogous category of mixed fish-and-vegetable soups with dairy, which are predominantly protein- and fat-dominant with very low carbohydrate content (1.68 g/100g). Carbohydrate content is too low to produce a meaningful glucose response. GL per serving calculated as: GI (35) × carbohydrate per serving (1.68 g/100g × 350 g serving = 5.88 g) / 100 = 2.1. University of Sydney GI Database consulted for analogous low-GI protein-dominant soup category.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 425 mg per 100 g with insufficient potassium and fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, avoid consuming the full soup broth, request reduced fish stock and salt during preparation, and supplement with potassium-rich vegetables to partially offset the high sodium load of 425 mg per 100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content of 1.68 g per 100 g with negligible glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried fish soup with milk is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content (1.68 g/100 g) and low glycaemic load (GL ≈ 2.1 per bowl); however, pairing with high-GI staples such as white rice or noodles should be minimised to preserve overall glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (425 mg/100 g) and deep-fried preparation contributing saturated fat and AGEs
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious of the high sodium content (425 mg/100 g) and the saturated fat contribution (~1.5 g/100 g) from deep-frying and evaporated milk; requesting steamed or poached fish and discarding excess broth can meaningfully reduce cardiovascular risk from this dish.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that may worsen joint inflammation despite omega-3 content from fish
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request steamed or lightly poached fish to reduce AGE formation, avoid consuming all the broth to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling, and be aware that tomato as a nightshade ingredient may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Fish Soup with Milk is a popular Singaporean hawker dish consisting of deep-fried sliced fish pieces and mixed vegetables served in a creamy, mildly flavoured soup broth enriched with evaporated milk. The fish — commonly batang (Spanish mackerel), snapper, or grouper — is first coated and deep-fried until golden and crisp, then added to a simmered stock base made from fish bones, ginger, and tomatoes. Evaporated milk is stirred in at the end to create a pale, creamy broth with a subtle sweetness and richness. The dish is typically garnished with fried shallots, spring onions, and sometimes preserved vegetables (cai xin or lettuce). Texturally, the fish is crisp on the outside and tender within, while the broth is smooth, warming, and mildly savoury. Nutritionally, the dish is low in carbohydrates (1.68 g/100 g) and moderate in protein (2.94 g/100 g) and fat (4.73 g/100 g), yielding approximately 53 kcal per 100 g. Sodium is elevated at 425 mg/100 g, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycaemic load is very low (GL ≈ 2.1 per bowl), making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments. The dish is commonly found at Singaporean hawker centres and food courts and is considered a comfort food suitable for all ages.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F03020015) at confidence 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean dishes. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is estimated from analogous low-carbohydrate fish soup category using the University of Sydney GI Database framework.
notes: Saturated fat estimated at approximately 1.5 g/100 g based on the fat profile typical of deep-fried fish with evaporated milk (HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this entry); this value is used as a reasonable approximation pending a more granular HPB breakdown. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish does not report these micronutrient and fatty acid fractions. Detailed fatty acid profile and micronutrient data for this traditional hawker preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. Input dietary_tags included 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' which are incorrect for a fish-based dish and have been removed. Region tag 'malaysia' from input has been corrected to 'Singapore' as this dish is canonically a Singaporean hawker dish. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database.
